coredata.dc:title: Investigation of the performance of direct forecasting strategy using machine learning in State-of-Charge prediction of Li-ion batteries exposed to dynamic loads
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Dineva, Adrienn
Csomós, Bence
Kocsis Sz., Szabolcs
Vajda, István
coredata.dc:description: On account of intense technological advances regarding Electric Vehicles, the state evaluation and prediction issues of Li-ion cells have become increasingly important for ensuring the competitiveness in terms of feasible performance and range. Albeit the wide investigation of various standard modelling and estimation techniques, only limited researches focus on their precision and applicability under heavy transient working conditions. This paper is concerned with Li-ion battery terminal voltage and State-of-Charge (SoC) prediction for two types of dynamic loads. Attention is focused on the investigation of the applicability of direct multi-step forecasting strategy in combination with Machine Learning. Beside that, a feature bank is composed of discharge profiles obtained at different C-rates. The set of discharge curves is proposed to complement the feature extraction, i.e. the additional historical data is considered for model building. Special care is devoted for the design of appropriate training data. Hence, a battery cell model is built for simulating intensive dynamic load scenarios in addition to the experimental setup. The cell model is validated by using measurement data. Results have demonstrated, that in case of WLTP-type discharge load of 0.3C-rate the forecasting performance is highly efficient on measurement data. Under dynamic loads of 1C-rate, or when small historical data is available, the application of feature bank improves the performance. We have obtained comprehensive results proving that the application of direct multi-step forecasting strategy using XGBoost represents a viable alternative to capture real-time the cell dynamics and predict the terminal voltage and SoC of Li-ion batteries exposed to dynamic loads.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion cell
State-of-Charge
Battery Management System (BMS)
Battery modelling
Electrochemical cell modell
WLTP
Dynamic load
Direct multi-step forecasting
Machine Learning (ML)
XGBoost
Prediction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Brief overview of the theoretical background of the prediction method using XGBoost
Prediction performance under heavy dynamic load

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Nemzeti Kutatási, Fejlesztési és Innovaciós Alap

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support provided by the FIEK program (Center for Cooperation Between Higher Education and the Industries) at the István Széchenyi University (Győr, Hungary) , GINOP-2.3.4-15-2016-00003 . The research is also supported by the UNKP-20-4 New National Excellence Program of the Ministry of Innovation and Technology from the source of the National Research, Development and Innovation (NRDI) Fund of Hungary .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The measurement setup.
Measured terminal voltage during WLTP-type discharge.
Measured current during WLTP-type discharge.
State-of-Charge of the cell during WLTP-type discharge.
Flowchart of the experiment design for the investigation of various strategies.
A basic 1D cell structure and its boundary conditions based on the DFN model. Some of the core material parameters are highlighted.

Equilibrium potential of NMC cathode with respect to Li metal.
Equilibrium potential of MCMB graphite anode with respect to Li metal.
Comparison of characteristics of measured and simulated terminal voltages during 1 C continuous-current discharge.
Comparison characteristics of measured and simulated terminal voltages during a WLTP test; no regenerative braking is considered.
Comparison of simulated and measured terminal voltage characteristics during an average of 0.5 C transient discharge.
Flowchart of the general boosting framework.
Forecasting performance with ± 0.3 [V] confidence interval.
225 time-step ahead forecasting of terminal voltage - zoomed.
Forecasting performance with ± 0.3 [V] confidence interval.
Prediction Performance using model trained on test data composed of measurement set and additional features. 225 time-step ahead forecasting of Terminal Voltage - zoomed.
Predicted vs. measured data under WLTP-type load.
Predicted vs. measured data under WLTP-type load using test set composed of measurement data plus additional feature bank.
Performance evaluation with respect to forecasting horizons under WLTP-type discharge load. Evolution of MAE values.
Performance evaluation with respect to forecasting horizons under WLTP-type discharge load. Evolution of MAPE values.

Forecasting SoC of the cell 225 s into the future using direct multistep forecasting strategy in combination with XGBoost. Dark violet dot-line: predicted SoC [ % ] with ± 2.5 [ % ] confidence interval; red line: calculated SoC from measurement data.

Forecasting SoC of the cell 225 s into the future using direct multistep forecasting strategy in combination with XGBoost using additional features during training. Dark violet dot-line: predicted SoC [ % ] with ± 2.5 [ % ] confidence interval; red line: calculated SoC from measurement data.
Simulated discharge datasets of intensive loads.
Performance evaluation with respect to forecasting horizons under intensive dynamic discharge load. Evolution of average MAE values of five cases.
Performance evaluation with respect to forecasting horizons under intensive dynamic discharge load. Evolution of average MAPE values of the five cases. Evolution of average MAPE values of five sets.

Simulation initial parameters applied in the DFN model. The letters “a” and “b” in the superscripts stand for “assumed” and “measured” respectively. The

head.ce:title: Investigation of the performance of direct forecasting strategy using machine learning in State-of-Charge prediction of Li-ion batteries exposed to dynamic loads

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: István Széchenyi University, Egyetem tér 1., H-9026 Györ, Hungary

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: István Széchenyi University, Egyetem tér 1., H-9026 Györ

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: István Széchenyi University, Egyetem tér 1., H-9026 Györ, Hungary

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ob́uda University, Bécsi út 96/b, H-1034 Budapest, Hungary

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ob́uda University, Bécsi út 96/b, H-1034 Budapest

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ob́uda University, Bécsi Street 96/b, H-1034 Budapest Hungary

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: On account of intense technological advances regarding Electric Vehicles, the state evaluation and prediction issues of Li-ion cells have become increasingly important for ensuring the competitiveness in terms of feasible performance and range. Albeit the wide investigation of various standard modelling and estimation techniques, only limited researches focus on their precision and applicability under heavy transient working conditions. This paper is concerned with Li-ion battery terminal voltage and State-of-Charge (SoC) prediction for two types of dynamic loads. Attention is focused on the investigation of the applicability of direct multi-step forecasting strategy in combination with Machine Learning. Beside that, a feature bank is composed of discharge profiles obtained at different C-rates. The set of discharge curves is proposed to complement the feature extraction, i.e. the additional historical data is considered for model building. Special care is devoted for the design of appropriate training data. Hence, a battery cell model is built for simulating intensive dynamic load scenarios in addition to the experimental setup. The cell model is validated by using measurement data. Results have demonstrated, that in case of WLTP-type discharge load of 0.3C-rate the forecasting performance is highly efficient on measurement data. Under dynamic loads of 1C-rate, or when small historical data is available, the application of feature bank improves the performance. We have obtained comprehensive results proving that the application of direct multi-step forecasting strategy using XGBoost represents a viable alternative to capture real-time the cell dynamics and predict the terminal voltage and SoC of Li-ion batteries exposed to dynamic loads.

ce:section.ce:para: As a result of the recent developments regarding Electric Vehicles (EVs), the prediction of terminal voltage and State-of-Charge (SoC) of Li-ion battery cells is recognized as being the most important issue for ensuring safe operation. Diagnostic and/or prediction methods that are able to timely assess the state of the battery cells/ pack have become critical components of on-board Battery Management System (BMS). Particularly, the SoC is defined as the maximum available charge that is present in a rechargeable cell. Improvement of the accuracy of SoC prediction methods and reducing the computational demand is still a challenging task and the subject of much research (see for e.g., [1,2]). The process of charging and discharging of batteries involve complex electrochemical reactions. The external environment has significant effect on the cell’s operating characteristics. The most common approaches include the so-called the look-up tables, model-based methods and data-driven prediction strategies. Physical model-based approaches are usually very complex and their on-line application is limited. For instance, physical modelling based technique is proposed in the literature [3] for SoC prediction which is able to predict the State-of-Charge with 3% error in case of a LiFePO cell, but normally such complex models can be obtained at the cost of huge efforts. Furthermore, most of the white-box methods neglect numerous inner electrochemical processes of the cell that are highly nonlinear and difficult to interpret, that may lead to significant degradation in prediction accuracy. However, much time can be saved by simulating test procedures in contrast to long measurement processes involving the charging and relaxation times. A vast amount of papers are addressing the model based approaches of batteries (see for e.g., [4–6]). Only few papers are subjected to examine the aspects of the dynamic properties of batteries. In case of Electric Vehicles (cars, buses, etc.), especially in urban traffic, batteries are exposed to markedly dynamic loads, with a small share of steady-state operation. For this reason, the effects of dynamic operation are of utmost importance. Equivalent Electrical Circuit (EEC) approach is widely accepted as fast and effective empirical alternative of complex modelling for battery performance prediction, that is capable for reducing the amount of physical measurement needs and computation demand. This is not necessarily done on a physical basis, though its aim is to establish a direct connection between the electrochemical processes in the cell and the circuit elements. These approaches are indispensable in complex, coupled space-based modelling tasks. For instance, the Li-ion battery EEC elements and corresponding processes are discussed in [7]. In practice, EEC is commonly parametrized with PPC (Pulse Power Current) signals. The accuracy of the model can be increased by increasing the number of R-C branches. Above five R-C branches the EEC model is applicable for diagnostic tasks and may provide more accurate SOC prediction, according to [8]. However, paper [9] highlights, that it may demand complicated nonstandard test procedures for the parametrization due to a large number of breakpoints. This increases complexity but also increases computation time as it was pointed out in [10]. In addition, nonlinear dependencies of the voltage on some electrochemical processes may be neglected [11]. At high frequencies, the impedance becomes inductive that must also be taken into account. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) shows the impedance of a cell as a function of frequency and temperature. Today, it is considered to be the most accurate method for determining cell impedance, as well as the most complex and costly measurement procedure. The inductive effect is caused by the geometry of the cell and the porosity of the electrode plates of the cell [12,13]. In addition, in [14] a new resistor-inductor network approach is proposed where the inductive modelling has no direct physical background. It can be seen that the high frequency properties of the cell can be well imaged by inductance. If the applied model neglects inductance, it follows that parameter identification cannot be extended to the range where the imaginary part of the impedance is positive (inductive). Therefore, such a model is legitimate if only the inductive nature of the battery is negligible. In many practical cases, high-frequency properties are of no practical significance, so inductances are often abandoned [15]. There is considerable amount of literature on the issue of inductances in battery modelling including as early, as well as the latest papers, for instance [8,16–20]. Much research has been conducted on linking the measurements to physical phenomena, but the time ranges of the system responses are quite wide; it can vary from microseconds to a few years. In case of batteries, current and temperature are most often considered as input signals and battery voltage and temperature, and also SoC and State-of-Health (SoH). There may be other output signals, such as the resistance or idle voltage, but from a dynamic point of view, these additional output signals provide little information, so we will not discuss them further here. According to [19] which presents pulsed discharge of NiMH battery, the internal and external parameters that affect the dynamic behaviour of the battery includes SoC, SoH, AC and DC resistance, design parameters as inner, and temperature, long-term and short-term history as outer parameters or factors. It can be seen, that most new SoC prediction approaches does not take into account such high-frequency regions and long-term history and altogether perform with limited accuracy. Problem of uninvestigated behaviours also present in data-driven prediction methods, where predicting the outcome of unseen data is performed by using a model fitted on a training dataset. The most common and popular techniques are Neural Networks (NN). Their main advantage is that they do not require prior knowledge of the model and are able to fit any nonlinear relationship due to their excellent approximation ability. For instance, a successful application of Long-Term Short-Term Memory (LSTM) NNs and Multi Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) in SoC prediction is addressed in [21]. The published data is obtained by repeated cycles of charging and discharging with C-rates of C/2 and C/4. In the traditional approach, performance of certain network types in the SoC prediction also presented in [22]. Despite of this interest, only few results can be found in the literature for the prediction of SoC of Li-ion cells exposed to heavy dynamic loads. Recent papers demonstrate the efficiency of prediction of various further methods, such as Kalman Filter, however most of them still rely on constant, PPC or standardized tests, usually with lower, e.g. C/10 discharge current [23]. Similarly, most of the presented results using Machine Learning (ML) approaches are built on training data obtained by standardized test procedures, because generating large training data covering the necessary typical or rare transient events is highly impractical or impossible to collect.

An even greater source of concern is that standardized tests cannot represent the dynamic behaviour of the Li-ion cell. More recent investigations proposes settings and designs for battery testing [11,24,25]. It has been shown that PPC tests are limited in bandwidth and in amplitude [24]. In a major advance in 2016, paper [24] recommends several reasons to apply the set of pulse-multisines which allows also the estimation of impedance and parametrization over a broader bandwidth in contrast to standardized tests.

According to the above briefly summarized state-of-the-art, the picture is thus still incomplete. Battery Management Systems (BMS) require models with lower complexity which are capable to produce reliable results for immediate decision making during real-time operation and early notification of potential terminal voltage collapses or prediction of dangerous drops in the terminal voltage without requiring complex models. Consequently, one of the greatest challenge of modern approaches is finding the trade-off between precision and complexity. Based on the antecedents, the purpose of this paper is to investigate the performance of terminal voltage prediction under specific dynamic loads. Since the thermal effects are not the scope of the current study, for approximating the SoC the terminal voltage method is applied at this stage of our research. With the aim of reducing the burden on training data, a direct multi-step-ahead time-series forecasting method in combination with ML strategy is adopted. Here, Extra Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) method is applied [26] to solve the terminal voltage prediction issue, since this method has already be proven to outperform common ML techniques [27]. Therefore, the main objectives of this paper include; recording specific operation conditions via cell measurements under WLTP-type loads; building a reliable cell model, which is capable of data generation for intensive load conditions; comparing the prediction performance in case of a these working conditions and investigating how the characteristics of training data influence the result.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. In Section 2 we present the experiment design and introduce the electrochemical cell model. We summarize the basic mathematical background of the XGBoost model and we discuss the performance metrics. In Section 3 we present the results of simulation investigations. Based on comparative analyses we derive the effect of dynamic loads on the prediction performance of the applied method. After, in Section 4 we draw some pertinent conclusions.

The battery used in the experiment is a LiNiMnCoO2 cell with 3.7 [V] and 2600 [mAh] capacity (Samsung ICR18650 26F). Since in the present investigation the thermal effects are neglected, the experiments are carried out at room temperature (23 [°C]).

The temperature deviation has a significant effect on several parameters of a Li-ion battery such as terminal voltage and available capacity which were the key factors in our present approach. Increasing temperature causes faster electrochemical reactions and smaller internal resistances. This results in a smaller overall terminal voltage drop and an increased available capacity [28,29]. This implies that the battery simulations and real measurements should be performed on several temperatures to provide valid temperature-dependent data for more realistic training. The temperature effect becomes important if the temperature difference is at least 10 °C from room temperature [28]. In the current paper, we considered at room temperature only.

The measurement setup is depicted in Fig. 1. Its units are as follows: power supply (Hameg HMP 4030), load (Elektro- Automatik EL3160-60 A), data logger (NI 9201), temperature sensors (LMT85LPG), thermal shock chamber (ESPEC LU-113) and a central computer responsible for test control with LabView software package. Voltage, current, and temperature data are saved for 100 ms to detect transient phenomena. Data which require less sampling are presented and processed after filtering and averaging.

First, a dataset is composed from measurement data containing various terminal voltage [V] - Time [s] discharge profiles. The measurements are carried out at constant current of 1C, 0.8C, 0.65C, 0.5C, 0.3C and 0.1C rates. In this paper we refer to this set of curves as feature bank.

After, measurement with WLTP (Worldwide Harmonized Light-Duty Vehicles Test Procedure) [30] type load is conducted of average 0.3C-rate, as mild dynamic load. The WLTP is the state-of-the-art driving scenario which is in agreement with the new European Union (EU) directive published in 2017 [31]. WLTP is an actualized version of NEDC to give more realistic and comparable emission characteristics between hybrid, electric and internal-combustion-engine vehicles. In addition, WLTP gives a more realistic driving pattern and emission data in EU than FUDS, DST and UDDS which are US standards. We used WLTP to fit our algorithm to the EU regulations. Even though the forecasting can be taught by any of the mentioned patterns, using other than WLTP may result in an unrealistic training data and so, decreased overall accuracy of SOC estimation. A detailed comparison of performances of different driving patterns was published in [32]. The WLTP discharge measurement data is depicted in Figs. 2–3 below:

From the measurement data the SoC is calculated by using Coulomb Counting method (see, Fig. 4) WLTP is chosen because this standard covers typical driving scenarios that can be learned as the most common driving features. The flowchart of the experiment design is depicted in Fig. 5.

The experiment covers at first investigating the performance of direct multi-step forecasting method combined with XGBoost in terminal voltage prediction in order to investigate how the adopted method is able to capture the cell dynamics under two distinguished classes of loads. The first is the above described WLTP-type load condition. For investigating the effect of WLTP-type load only measurement data is used. Second, simulated intensive stochastic load profile is applied via simulation on the electrochemical cell model for obtaining data for the investigation of the intensive load class. The intensive load class covers heavy dynamic load profiles without any seasonality.

In case of both types of loads two types of prediction strategies are applied. First, the models are trained on a truncated dataset only from the historical data, and the rest is applied for testing. In the second case, the models are trained also on the truncated historical datasets but the training set is complemented with a feature bank which contains the measured discharge curves of various discharge rates. The contribution of this work focuses on also the comparison of the performance of the four prediction strategies under the distinguished load types and investigate whether the application of the feature bank has effect on the performance. The appropriate strategies are determined by the results of terminal voltage predictions, since it reflects better the model’s ability to capture the cell’s dynamic behaviour. The input variables of the models are the measured/simulated terminal voltage and load current history data and calculated (or alternatively, the predicted) SoC history data. Here, the temperature input data is considered as a constant input. In case of real-time applications, these inputs can be collected in a sliding window. The historical data can be taken into account in the lookback window of a forecaster. Various lengths of forecasting horizons are also compared in order to find the proper setting depending on the available predictors. In all cases windows are applied for creating lagged data and the performance is evaluated in all the different time horizons. The final prediction is the combination of the outcome of the models at each horizon (see, Fig. 5)

The forecasting is basically refer to predicting one time-step into the future. However, in practical application the need of longer prediction may arise. For instance, it is necessary to predict the abrupt harmful terminal voltage drop and to predict the fluctuation of terminal voltage for energy management purposes in an on-board system. In addition, the BMS requires a short time to predict and intervene. Generally, predicting the SoC for longer time into-the-future in real environment may require detailed knowledge about the future load currents and the nonlinear relationships with the temperature. In a data driven sense this means a huge data demand. Instead, the forecasting approach can built up on the immediate history of limited samples, or the past data complemented with some additional features which reflect the cell dynamics. In the present work only the past load is known and the future load is assumed unknown, only its type is defined (WLTP-type or intensive stochastic).

The finite-element environment is an effective platform to rapidly generate tremendous battery data for AI training. We have developed a finite-element battery model and its applicability has been verified by three types of test patterns such as 1 C constant-current discharge, WLTP and intensive transient tests. A common and reliable electrochemical model used in finite element analysis is based on the work of Doyle et al. [33] that is called Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model. It consist of charge and mass balance equations for both the solid (electrode) and liquid (electrolyte) materials which describe the main operating characteristics of the cell. Even though the DFN model is suitable for battery modelling in 1D, 2D and 3D, the best compromise between computation effort and model resolution can be achieved by implementing the model in pseudo 2D environment. The DFN model implements the battery dynamics through concentrations and potentials and it gives a good approximation of cell behaviour up to 1 C cell currents [34]. The DFN model is well-favoured in finite element analysis (FEA) due to its low computational costs and good performance under moderate load conditions [35]. In pseudo-2D models, the electrodes are modelled by the framework of porous electrode theory which treats the electrodes as homogeneous multiphase system [36]. On Fig. 6, a typical pseudo-2D structure of a Li-ion cell can be seen.

In this point-of-view, all the particles are spherical and the volumes of electrodes are averaged. The electrode’s porosity has been implemented parametrically. The Li-ion transport is considered only in direction of the electrode thickness. The presentation of mathematical apparatus used in this paper can be found in [37] for a Li-ion cell. The partial differential equations (PDEs) can be grouped according to the following structures: charge conservation in solid electrodes; charge conservation in the electrolyte, in the bulk and inside the electrode pores; mass conservation in the solid active material; mass conservation in the electrolyte; electrochemical kinetics at the electrode-electrolyte interface.

The charge conservation in the solid phase along only the x axis can be written according to Ohm’s law:

e
( 1 − α )
F μ
R T
−
e
( α )
F μ
R T
where
ι
0
is the exchange current density, α stands for the charge transfer coefficient and μ represents the overpotential. As it is mentioned above, Eqs. (1), (7), (11), (14) and (16) are the main governing equations of the DFN model with the corresponding boundary conditions. Furthermore, the pseudo-2D DFN model should be specified to the cell inspected by a set of identical electrochemical and geometrical parameters. These parameters can be grouped into two groups such as initial values and those which change over time. The list of initial values can be seen in Table 1.

The assumed values have been adopted from Carelli et al. [38] and Vaoen et al. [39] and the calculations are based on EIS measurement and equivalent circuit modelling technique. In pseudo-DFN battery model, the functions of
D
l , 0
,
∂
f
+ −
∂ c
and
κ
l
have a cardinal impact on the battery behaviour. Even though these parameters depend on the cell SOC, temperature, aging in reality, only their function of concentration
c
l
has been considered in this work in order to reduce computational complexity of the system. The functions of
D
l , 0
(
c
l
)
∂
f
+ −
∂ c
and
κ
l
(
c
l
)
are determined by the composition of the applied electrolyte. Although the exact electrolyte composition is usually unknown in case of a specific commercial cell, a commonly used electrolyte is LiPF6 in an organic solvent. The alkyl carbonate in these solutions are typically made of a mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC) and/or ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) with different volume ratios. Our pseudo-2D DFN model has been specified by a parameter set of an electrolyte containing LiPF6 salt in PC:EC:DMC 10:27:63 v/v % solution adopted from [39], which investigates a commercial LiPF6-type electrolyte. These functions can be seen in Figs. 7(a), 7(b) and 8.

The electrochemical behavior of the inspected cell has been implemented both parametrically based on the parameter set shown in Table 1 and by functions of the electrodes and electrolytes presented in Figs. 7–10. In order to verify our electrochemical battery model, we have performed three types of verification tests where the simulation and measurements have been evaluated and compared. The results can be seen in Figs. 11–13.

First, a normal discharge with 1C constant-current is applied in order to examine the diffusion-related characteristics of the cell. In Fig. 11, the appearing small deviation between the measurement and simulation outcome has been introduced by the parameter uncertainties. Then, the cell has been undergone two types of dynamical load pattern which should evaluate the accuracy of battery model during fast transient operation. A realistic WLTP dynamic load pattern is applied, since it is a state-of-the-art scenario dedicated to simulate both urban and high-speed driving. The regenerative braking has been neglected in this work. The average load current of the WLTP test profile is 0,75 C. The result of the WLTP test is shown in Fig. 12, the modelled terminal voltage accurately follows the measured data until the pattern reaches the high-speed (high-current) regime at about 1200 [s]. A noticeable difference can be observed between the measured and simulated data in the time interval of 1200-1750 [s]. It is due to the validity limit of the DFN model is violated by currents applied above 1 C threshold in the given regime. However, the difference between the measured and simulated terminal voltage shows a small RMS error of 0.0416 and an appropriate fit of
R
2
= 0 . 9798
. We are also interested in investigating the performance of the DFN model during slowly-changing discharge rates when an average of 0.5C dynamic current profile is applied. The evaluation results can be seen in Fig. 12. It can be seen in Fig. 12, that the simulated transient voltage smoothly fits on the measured data resulting RMSE of 0.0452 and
R
2
of 0.9562. Based on the verification results, the applicability of our DFN model for data generation is clearly observable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Brief overview of the theoretical background of the prediction method using XGBoost
ce:section.ce:para: Standard Machine Learning-based methods for predicting the State-of-Charge apply regression models that are usually built off-line on training data neglecting dynamic operation conditions. Another drawback is that the prediction is made on the basis of the actual measured inputs, i.e. one time-step outcome instance is predicted at once. In this paper we propose to adopt a direct forecasting [40] approach using XGBoost method. The present study considers the task of terminal voltage prediction as a multivariate multistep-ahead time series prediction issue, and the Machine Learning models for time-series data may perform in a sliding window during real-time operation. This reduces the data requirement and holds several benefits, such as easy online training, flexible fitting to specific conditions and predicting various time-lengths into the future. The direct forecasting approach covers building individual models for each horizon [40]. The XGboost method is a decision-tree-based ensemble [41] Machine Learning algorithm which applies a gradient boosting framework. The principle of ensemble methods is that they apply a set of weak learners and aggregate the outputs all of these weak model into a stronger final prediction. The XGboost algorithm provides several benefits, since it was developed in order to enhance the computational properties of other boosting methods (such as AdaBoost). The general framework of the gradient boosting is depicted in Fig. 14.

XGBoost is a gradient boosting strategy introduced in [26], that covers a set of decision tree models as learners and provides a modified boosting calculation method. Formally [26]:
(17)
y
ˆ
= θ
(
x
i
)
=
∑
n = 1
N
f
n
(
x
i
)
,
in which
θ
(
x
i
)
represents the XGBoost model with
n = 1 , … , N
number of
f
n
( ⋅ )
weak learners. The efficiency of this method relies on applying a Newton boosting instead of standard gradient boosting [26]. The optimal parameters are found via minimizing the V loss function given by Eq. (18)
(18)
V
( θ )
=
∑
i = 1
k
v
(
y
ˆ
i
,
y
i
)
+
∑
n = 1
N
R
(
f
n
)
(19)
R
(
f
n
)
= γ T =
1
2
λ ‖ w
‖
2
.

In Eq. (18), (19) the v function denotes the cost function of the
i
th
predicted
y
ˆ
i
and target
y
i
values and
R
(
f
n
)
stands for the regularization term responsible for penalizing the n th model’s complexity. The symbol k represents the sample size and T is the number of leaf nodes, while γ is a control parameter related to tree structure and λ is a regularization control parameter. In order to avoid the difficulties of solving the problem in Euclidean space an
f
t
term is added [26]. After, the loss function is formulated as follows:
(20)
V
t
=
∑
i = 1
k
v
(
y
ˆ
i
,
y
i
t − 1
+
f
t
(
x
i
)
)
+ R
(
f
t
)

The term
y
i
t
in Eq. (20) denotes the prediction of the i th observation in the t th iteration and
f
t
is the corresponding model. Afterwards, a second order Taylor series expansion on
v
(
y
ˆ
i
,
y
i
t − 1
+
f
t
(
x
i
)
)
is performed, for the sake of fast and convenient calculations [26]. Then, Eq. (21) is obtained [26]:
(21)
g
i
f
t
(
x
i
)
+
1
2
h
i
f
t
2
(
x
i
)
,
where
(22)
g
i
=
∂ v
(
y
i
,
y
ˆ
i
t − 1
)
y
i

ˆ
,
(23)
h
i
=
∂
2
v
(
y
i
,
y
ˆ
i
t − 1
)
y
i
t − 1
ˆ
and
(24)
V
t
=
∑
i = 1
k
λ
y
i

ˆ
i
t − 1
+
g
i

t
(

i
)
+
1
2
h
i
f
t
2
(
x
i
)
+ R
(
f
t
)

According to Eq. (24) the weights of the leaves are given by Eq. (25) below [26].
(25)
w
j
= −
∑
i ∈
I
j
g
i
∑
i ∈
I
j
h
i
+ λ
In the above expression I represents the example set of the j th leaf node. From Eq. (25) the following objective function can be deduced for the j th node for q structure, as
(26)
V
˜
( t )
( q )
= −
1
2
∑
j = 1
T
∑
i ∈
I
j
g
i
2
∑
i ∈
I
j
h
i
+ λ
+ γ T .
Then, the G information gain is formulated in Eq. (27) as
(27)
G =
1
2
∑
i ∈
I
j L
g
i
2
2
∑
i ∈
I
j R
h
i
+ λ
+
∑
i ∈
I
j R
g
i
2
∑
i ∈
I
j L
h
i
+ λ
+
∑
i ∈
I
j
g
i
2
∑
i ∈
I
j
h
i
+ λ
− γ ,
in which
I
j L
and
I
j R
denote the example sets of the left and right leaf nodes after splitting [26].

According to the original paper of Chen and Guestrin [26], a column block structure is proposed for storing the data and helping parallelization. In contrast, the standard greedy approach stores the entire data in one block and performs the search linearly. The application of blocks or in-memory units significantly reduce the time costs, since getting the data into sorted order is one of the most time-consuming step of the procedure. The blocks can be distributed and stored on disks making it suitable for out-of-core settings. The complexity of the XGBoost algorithm with the block structure is
O
( N d
x
0
+
x
0
l o g B )
, where
x
0
stands for the missing entries in the training set, B is the maximum number of rows in each block, d is the depth of the tree and N is the maximum number of models [26]. Altogether, these attractive properties make this method suitable for applying in onboard BMS systems.

Since the evaluation of forecasting accuracy is reliable on unseen data, we perform the training on truncated datasets. After, the prediction performance is evaluated on the removed instances by applying most-commonly used metrics, such as Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE). The formulae of the metrics are given by Eqs. (28)–(30).
(28)
RMSE =
∑
i = 1
n
(
y
i
−
y
i
ˆ
)
2
n
(29)
MAE =
1
2
∑
i = 1
n
(
y
i
y
i
ˆ
)
(30)
MAPE =
∑
i = 1
n
1
2
|
y
i
−
y
i
ˆ
y
i
|
,
where
y
i
(
i = 1 , … , n
) are the measured datapoints and
y
i
ˆ
(
i = 1 , … , n
) are the predicted terminal voltage values [V] at the i th timestep [s]. Percentage errors, such as MAPE (or alternatively, the SMAPE (Symmetric Mean Absolute Error) is also well-known, but less recommended [42]) are scale independent metrics and frequently applied for forecast performance evaluation. RMSE and MAE are scale-dependent measures which are advantageous, also for finding the best one of several models. However, it is worth to note that, in practice the dynamic load varies stochastically and may encounter sudden events, that can degrade the performance. Thus, the results need to be interpreted with care.

In this section the performances of the four modelling strategies depicted in Fig. 5 are compared. The performances of the models are evaluated based on the terminal voltage data, in order to reveal the prediction power by catching the cell dynamics in case of the considered dynamic conditions. Then, the performances are evaluated in State-of-Charge prediction of the WLTP-type measurement data.
In all cases the forecasting horizons are determined in 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200 and 225 s into the future. The simulation investigations are carried out by using R version of 4.0.1.

In this section the performances of two modelling strategies are evaluated. First, the terminal voltage is predicted using models built on 1800 s long measured input data of terminal voltage, load current and temperature. After, the SoC is determined by Coulomb Counting method. Next, a new model is built by creating additional predictor variable from the calculated SoC data. Then, the SoC is predicted 225 time-steps into the future. Forecasting performed on WLTP measurement data involves applying a truncated dataset (see, Fig. 2). The last 225 [s] of the full measurement data is predicted by adopting the trained XGBoost models. Then, the direct strategy adopts the trained forecasting models to predict the outcome. The results can be seen in Figs. 15–22. In the first case the lookback window lengths are stated in
η 1 = 600
[ s ]
and
η 2 = 60
[ s ]
, which takes the features from one-third of the WLTP’s duration and from every minute. In case of applying the additional historical discharge profile from the feature bank, the parameters are determined to be
η 1 = 1600
[ s ]
and
η 2 = 60
[ s ]
. It can be apparent from Figs. 15–18 that both strategies are efficient. The performance is considered to be acceptable below 0.5 [V] deviation. It is notable from Figs. 19–20, that the application of feature bank degrades the performance. The graphs in Figs. 21 report also, that the forecasting results using model built on only the WLTP discharge data provide better accuracy. The results indicate, that the training data contains enough features and the model is able to catch the cell’s behaviour. It can be observed in Figs. 21–22 that with increasing length of the forecasting horizon the performance slightly degrades. A peak at about fifty seconds long horizon (see, Figs. 21–22) may indicate a forecasting error. After the initial transient, the prediction performance becomes smoother.

According to the comparative analyses of model performances presented above, it has become clear, that the XGBoost model using a direct multistep forecasting strategy is highly suitable for capturing the cell dynamics. The results demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed approach in SoC forecasting into the future using past data during WLTP-type discharge loads. Here, the measurement data and calculated SoC data is used both for training and testing. The SoC derived from measurements and Coulomb counting method can be considered a linear section (93%) within the prediction horizon, which is used for evaluating the outcome. Alternatively, by utilizing the DFN model for data generation, the SOC calculation of past data recorded in a sliding window may also be replaced by an additional offline-trained ML model for SoC calculation, which is expected to be especially beneficial when temperature fluctuations are present.

Figs. 23–24 display the SoC prediction performances. It can be observed in Fig. 23 that during WLTP-type discharge load the prediction provides approximately 1% average error in State-of-Charge. In case of the second strategy, when the training set contains the additional features, the prediction error increases (see, Fig. 24). Further work needs to be carried out to establish the appropriate feature set to be taken into account in order to enhance the performance in case of the second strategy. In addition, future investigations on the hyperparameter optimization is recommended, which could significantly affect the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction performance under heavy dynamic load
ce:section.ce:para:
By turning now to at the second case, we investigate the performance during intensive dynamic load which is a stochastic of nature. Hence, in order to assess the performance more precisely, we provide average metric values of five prediction results on different sets
(
v
1
,
v
2
,
v
3
,
v
4
,
v
5
)
(see, Table 2). The datasets used for testing are depicted in Fig. 25. All the sets of discharge profiles of intensive dynamic loads are obtained by simulation described in Section 2.2. The multi-step forecaster model then applied to predict unseen intervals of 10–225 s from each dynamic phase. In case of using feature bank, the following time intervals are removed and the data is shifted; in the first set (
v
1
) from 1:20:31 to 1:24:15, in case of set
v
2
from 1:08:01 to 1:11:45, in set
v
3
from 1:00:00 to 1:03:44, in set
v
4
from 1:16:16 to 1:20:00 and on set
v
5
the last 225 s are eliminated.

The lookback windows are determined in
η 1 = 600
[ s ]
and
η 2 = 60
[ s ]
in the first case and
η 1 = 1800
[ s ]
and
η 2 = 60
[ s ]
in case of using feature bank. According to trial-error investigations, these values have been found to be the most suitable. This stresses the importance of using features in model building which fit to the type of the load type. There were no significant differences between 1500 and 2000 s long lookback windows in case of using feature bank. These investigations support that in case of intensive stochastic loads, the proposed size of the lookback window may vary between 1500-2000 s, which is able to catch the battery’s dynamics, as well as the discharge characteristics. For the sake of comparison, the best performing lookback windows for mild loads is one-third of the full length of the WLTP cycle with about at 100 [s] long horizon according to the
M A E
and also the
M A P E
metrics depicted in Fig. 21. It is also observable, that the method is suitable for predicting longer horizons when WLTP-type loads are present and for predicting shorter horizons in case of intensive dynamic loads (see Figs. 26 and 27).

In this paper an attempt is made to approach the terminal voltage and State-of-Charge prediction problem from the perspective of non-standard dynamic loads. This viewpoint allows us to have a more comprehensive view of the real needs of EV applications and to find the best-fitting strategy. The state-of-the-art SoC prediction methods rely on training models on standardized, constant or impulse discharge tests. Since such data do not contain information on the battery dynamics on high frequency range that needs attention in EV applications, we propose to build the data-driven prediction models built on measured and simulated data of specific and intensive load scenarios. Attention is focused on the adoption of direct forecasting strategy using XGBoost technique in terminal voltage and SoC forecasting. In comparison to the standard ML-based and EEC models, the discussed prediction approach does not require to learn all the possible performance conditions and scenarios, thus more appropriate for real-time application in BMSs. On the basis of our investigations, we propose to utilize the physical cell modelling for fast data generation that allows exhaustive investigation of various prediction methods and their proper settings in the design phase. It can be concluded that composing the train set with features extracted from measurement and/or simulated data is far effective in terms of spanned frequency domain of operation, data- and computation need. When the size of the available training set is small, or when heavy dynamic loads occur we recommend the application of feature bank of terminal voltage - time curves for creating time lags for improving prediction performance. The results of investigations have shown that XGBoost in combination with direct multi-step forecasting strategy allows excellent performance in terminal voltage and SoC prediction of dynamic regions of operations including rare events. Moreover, it may give a sufficient basis for BMS applications. In addition, the investigations underline that this strategy allows adequate performance between 50 - 150 seconds into the future in case of dynamic loads, while ensures reliable performance even above 200 seconds long horizons in case of WLTP-type loads. Future work will explore the performance on partially charged cells at various temperatures and applying measurement data of dynamic charge/discharge tests as well. To conclude, based on our results, it is expected that the adoption of XGBoost model will contribute to further enhance the performance of battery management systems by mapping the cell dynamics more accurately in real driving scenarios.

Adrienn Dineva: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigations, Software, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review and editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Bence Csomós: Methodology, Software, Investigations, Validation, Writing - review and editing. Szabolcs Kocsis Sz.: Data curation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review and editing. István Vajda: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support provided by the FIEK program (Center for Cooperation Between Higher Education and the Industries) at the István Széchenyi University (Győr, Hungary), GINOP-2.3.4-15-2016-00003. The research is also supported by the UNKP-20-4 New National Excellence Program of the Ministry of Innovation and Technology from the source of the National Research, Development and Innovation (NRDI) Fund of Hungary .

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State-of-charge estimation for li-ion batteries: A more accurate hybrid approach

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. S. Misyris, D. I. Doukas, T. A. Papadopoulos, D. P. Labridis, V. G. Agelidis, State-of-charge estimation for li-ion batteries: A more accurate hybrid approach, IEEE Transactions on Energy Conversion 34 (1) (2019) 109–119.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Data driven nonlinear identification of li-ion battery based on frequency domain nonparametric analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Relan, Y. Firouz, J.-M. Timmermans, J. Schoukens, Data driven nonlinear identification of li-ion battery based on frequency domain nonparametric analysis, IEEE Trans. on Cont. Sys. Tech. 25 Issue 5. (2018) 1–8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A realistic model for battery state of charge prediction in energy management simulation tool

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Horman, M. Kortenaar, J. Hurink, G. Smit, A realistic model for battery state of charge prediction in energy management simulation tool, Energy 171 (2019) 205–217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State of charge estimation based on a simplified electrochemical model for a single LiCoO2 battery and battery pack

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, L. Wang, L. Chao, M. Pecht, State of charge estimation based on a simplified electrochemical model for a single licoo2 battery and battery pack, Energy 133 (2017) 572–583.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical model-based state of charge and capacity estimation for a composite electrode lithium-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Bartlett, J. Marcicki, S. Onori, G. Rizzoni, X. Yang, Electrochemical model-based state of charge and capacity estimation for a composite electrode lithium-ion battery, IEEE Control Syst. Mag. 24 (2016) 384–399.
G. L. Plett, Battery Modeling, Artech House, 685 Canton Street, Norwood, MA 02062, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review—Dynamic models of li-ion batteries for diagnosis and operation: A review and perspective

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U. Krewer, F. Rder, E. Harinath, R. D. Braatz, B. Bedrftig, R. Findeisen, Reviewdynamic models of li-ion batteries for diagnosis and operation: A review and perspective, J. Electrochem. Soc. 165 (16) (2018) A3656A3673.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of lithium-ion battery models based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U. Westerhoff, K. Kurbach, F. Lienesch, M. Kurrat, Analysis of lithium-ion battery models based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, Energy Technology 4 (12) (2016) 16201630.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple, effective lead-acid battery modeling process for electrical system component selection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. A. Jackey, A simple, effective lead-acid battery modeling process for electrical system component selection, in: SAE Technical Paper, SAE International, 2007. doi:10.4271/2007-01-0778. URL https://doi.org/10.4271/2007-01-0778

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: G. Alexander, H. Anton, Parametrization of a simplified physical battery model, in: Proc. 13th Int. Model. Conf., Regensburg, Germany, March 4–6, 2019, Vol. 157, 2019, pp. 215–220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study of the influence of measurement timescale on internal resistance characterisation methodologies for lithium-ion cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Barai, K. Uddin, W. Widanage, A. McGordon, P. Jennings, Study of the influence of measurement timescale on internal resistance characterisation methodologies for lithium-ion cells, Scientific Reports 8 (21). doi:https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-017-18424-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Guidelines for the characterization of the internal impedance of lithium-ion batteries in PHM algorithms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Prez, M. Benavide, H. Rozas, S. Seria, M. Orchard, Guidelines for the characterization of the internal impedance of lithium-ion batteries in phm algorithms, Int. J. Progn. Heal. Manag. 9 (Special Issue 9) (2018) 1–10.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-ion battery models: A comparative study and a model-based powerline communication

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Saidani, F. X. Hutter, R. G. Scurtu, W. Braunwarth, J. N. Burghartz, Lithium-ion battery models: A comparative study and a model-based powerline communication, Adv. Radio Sci. 15 (2015) 8391.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel resistor-inductor network-based equivalent circuit model of lithium-ion batteries under constant-voltage charging condition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yang, Y. Cai, C. Pan, , C. Mi, A novel resistor-inductor network-based equivalent circuit model of lithium-ion batteries under constant-voltage charging condition, Appl. Energy 254 (2019) 113726.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization of high-power lithium-ion batteries by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. II: Modeling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Andre, M. Keiler, K. Steiner, H. Waltz, T. Soczka-Guth, D. Sauer, Characterization of high-power lithium-ion batteries by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. ii: Modeling, Journal of Power Sources 196 (12) (2011) 53495356.

sb:reference.sb:comment: Ch. 6. Diagnosis of the internal resistance of an automotive lead-acid battery by the implementation of model invalidation-based approach: Application of crankability estimation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Sabatier, M. Cugnet, S. Laurelle, S. Grugeon, I. Chanteur, B. Sahut, A. Oustaloup, J.-M. Tarascon, Electrical Machines Diagnosis, John Wiley & Sons, 2011, Ch. 6. Diagnosis of the Internal Resistance of an Automotive Lead-Acid Battery by the Implementation of Model Invalidation-based Approach: Application of Crankability Estimation, pp. 167–192.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A physically-based equivalent circuit model for the impedance of a LiFePO 4 /Graphite 26650 cylindrical cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Scipioni, P. S. Jrgensen, C. Graves, J. Hjelm, S. H. Jensen, A physically-based equivalent circuit model for the impedance of a lifepo 4 /graphite 26650 cylindrical cell, J. Electrochem. Soc. 164 (9) (2017) A2017A2030.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Equivalent circuit model parameters extraction for lithium ion batteries using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Vyroubal, T. Kazda, Equivalent circuit model parameters extraction for lithium ion batteries using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, J. Energy Storage 15 (2018) 23–31.
A. Jossen, Fundamentals of battery dynamics, J. Power Sources 154 (2) (2006) 530538.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Estimation of state of charge for lithium-ion batteries - A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Attanayaka, J. Karunadasa, K. Hemapala, Estimation of state of charge for lithium-ion batteries - a review, AIMS Energy 7 (2) (2019) 186–210.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A. Khalid, A. Sundararajan, I. Acharya, I. Sarwat, Prediction of li-ion battery state of charge using multilayer perceptron and long short-term memory models, in: Proceedings of the 2019 IEEE Transportation Electrification Conference and Expo, ITEC, 19–21 June 2019, Detroit, MI USA, 2019, pp. 2377–5483.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Machine learning-based lithium- ion battery capacity estimation exploiting multi-channel charging profiles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Choi, K. Ryu, S. adn Park, H. Kim, Machine learning-based lithium- ion battery capacity estimation exploiting multi-channel charging profiles, IEEE Access 7 (2019) 75143–75152.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State-of-charge estimation for lithium-ion batteries using a Kalman filter based on local linearization
